schema.description: Catalogue of every visual asset already in our possession (committed to repo or hot-linked from a known free source) plus a wishlist of high-priority gaps to be filled by B2 (period/place context imagery).

fields.file_path: path relative to repo root, OR null if external
fields.url: canonical URL if external/hot-linked, OR null if local
fields.kind: primary-evidence | derived-evidence | site-illustration | hero-background | process-artefact | wishlist
fields.person_ids: array of family.json person IDs the asset depicts/concerns
fields.date: ISO-ish date the original document was created (NOT when we found it)
fields.place: where the original document was created
fields.license: license/rights statement (PD-old, CC-BY-SA-4.0, Crown ©, IWM Non-Commercial, IrishGenealogy.ie terms, Wikimedia file page, family copyright, unknown)
fields.caption: short caption suitable for use on the site
fields.credit: byline/attribution string to display under the image
fields.used_on_pages: list of HTML pages currently embedding this asset
fields.thumbnail: path or URL of a thumbnail variant if separately stored

summary.process_artefact: many (gitignored or archival only — not catalogued individually)
summary.key_gap: We have ZERO family-personal photographs (no portraits of Thomas, Bridget, Catherine, Patrick, Elizabeth, Joseph, the Greene siblings, or any group photos). Every photograph on the site is currently either generic Unsplash mood imagery, public-domain place imagery, or a primary-source document scan. Mark/Claire's family albums (if they exist) are the single biggest unrealised source.
summary.b2_status: B2 in progress: 6 of 7 hot-linked/wishlist images now self-hosted under images/illustrations/. Portarlington Main Street geograph file deleted from Wikimedia and replaced with c.1900 Emo Park PD image.
summary.b4_status: integrated 2026-04: 11 assets wired into evidence-strips + inline lightbox figures on catherine/elizabeth/portarlington/military

primary_evidence.place: Roman Catholic Church of Holy Cross, Dundrum, Co. Dublin (Rathdown RD)
primary_evidence.source: General Register Office of Ireland (GRO) civil marriage register, image published by IrishGenealogy.ie record cima-1271568. Original PDF at research-results/r1-tg-bc-marriage-1922/register.pdf
primary_evidence.license: IrishGenealogy.ie — Crown © Government of Ireland; permitted for personal & non-commercial research use under irishgenealogy.ie/en/usage-of-data
primary_evidence.caption: The 1922 marriage register entry for Thomas Greene and Bridget Clarke at Holy Cross, Dundrum (entry 89 — last on page).
primary_evidence.credit: © Government of Ireland — General Register Office; via IrishGenealogy.ie
primary_evidence.rights_notes: Display permitted; commercial reuse requires permission. Always cite IrishGenealogy.ie record ID cima-1271568.

primary_evidence.source: GRO civil birth register, Duleek RD entry 107, registered by James Kelly. Image via IrishGenealogy.ie record civbi-9688899. Original PDF at research-results/r2-bridget-birth-1891/register.pdf
primary_evidence.license: IrishGenealogy.ie — Crown © Government of Ireland; permitted for personal & non-commercial research use
primary_evidence.caption: Bridget Clarke's 1891 birth register entry — the document that revealed Catherine's maiden name as CLINTON and named Patrick Clarke as Bridget's father.
primary_evidence.credit: © Government of Ireland — General Register Office; via IrishGenealogy.ie
primary_evidence.rights_notes: The single most important record in the Clarke side of the family.

primary_evidence.source: GRO civil marriage register; IrishGenealogy.ie record cima-2861090. PDF at research-results/r3-pc-cm-marriage-1875/register.pdf
primary_evidence.caption: Patrick Clarke × Catherine Kavanagh née McKenna, Navan 1875 — archived to demonstrate why this is NOT our family (different parties, wrong town, wrong maiden name vs the 1891 birth-register's CLINTON).
primary_evidence.credit: © Government of Ireland — General Register Office; via IrishGenealogy.ie
primary_evidence.rights_notes: Display only as a 'false lead — ruled out' worked-example, never as evidence of our line.

primary_evidence.place: House 3, Mullaghfin, Duleek DED, Co. Meath
primary_evidence.source: National Archives of Ireland — 1901 Census of Ireland, Form A. Reel image at http://www.census.nationalarchives.ie/reels/nai001112178/ . Local screenshot at research-results/clarke_duleek_1901.png
primary_evidence.license: © National Archives of Ireland — free use for non-commercial purposes with citation
primary_evidence.caption: The 1901 Census Form A for House 3 Mullaghfin: Catherine Clarke (40, widow) heads the household with all six surviving Clarke children — Rose, Patrick, Mary, Jane, Bridget (age 9), and Agnes.
primary_evidence.credit: © National Archives of Ireland — 1901 Census, Mullaghfin, Duleek, Co. Meath
primary_evidence.rights_notes: Always cite NAI and link to the public NAI page.

primary_evidence.source: National Archives of Ireland — 1901 Census DED-level browse page. Local screenshot.
primary_evidence.license: © National Archives of Ireland — free non-commercial use
primary_evidence.caption: The Mullaghfin DED household-list page from 1901, showing the Clarke household in the wider neighbourhood context.
primary_evidence.rights_notes: Useful for showing neighbours and immediate community.

primary_evidence.source: National Archives of Ireland — 1911 Census of Ireland, Form A. Local screenshot.
primary_evidence.license: © National Archives of Ireland — free non-commercial use
primary_evidence.caption: The 1911 Census record for Catherine Clarke (now 55, widow) at the same House 3 Mullaghfin — by now most of the children have moved out.
primary_evidence.credit: © National Archives of Ireland — 1911 Census, Mullaghfin, Duleek, Co. Meath
primary_evidence.rights_notes: Last documentary trace of Catherine alive.

primary_evidence.place: Joseph Dollard household, Dublin (Wellington Quay area)
primary_evidence.source: National Archives of Ireland — 1911 Census of Ireland, Form B No. 142, enumerated 2 April 1911 by Robert J. Holmes. Filename is misleading — this is in fact Bridget Clarke's 1911 census entry as Cook in the Dollard household, NOT Catherine's.
primary_evidence.license: © National Archives of Ireland — free non-commercial use
primary_evidence.caption: Bridget Clarke at age 19, working as Cook (Domestic) in the household of magistrate Joseph Dollard (a printer-master and JP) in Dublin — eleven years before her marriage to Thomas Greene.
primary_evidence.credit: © National Archives of Ireland — 1911 Census, Dublin (Dollard household)
primary_evidence.rights_notes: FILE-RENAME-RECOMMENDED: rename catherine_clarke_1911.png → bridget_clarke_dollard_1911.png in a future cleanup commit (the current name is inaccurate).

site_illustration.place: Biberach an der Riß, Baden-Württemberg, Germany
site_illustration.caption: Biberach an der Riß — the German town where the WW2 internment camp Ilag VB held British civilian internees evacuated from the Channel Islands, including (by family tradition) Elizabeth Harris.
site_illustration.rights_notes: Self-hosted from Wikimedia Commons original. File page: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Biberach-012.jpg

site_illustration.source: Imperial War Museum (IWM) collection D24599, via Wikimedia Commons
site_illustration.caption: Liberation of the Channel Islands, May 1945 — the end of the five-year German occupation under which Elizabeth Harris (then in Jersey) lived.
site_illustration.credit: Imperial War Museum (IWM D24599) via Wikimedia Commons
site_illustration.rights_notes: Self-hosted from IWM scan via Wikimedia. Always credit IWM D24599. (Earlier hot-link path had become stale on Wikimedia — fixed by this self-host.)

site_illustration.source: Wikimedia Commons — File:Leinster Regiment Cap Badge.jpg
site_illustration.caption: Cap badge of the Prince of Wales's Leinster Regiment (Royal Canadians) — the Irish line infantry regiment whose service area covered the family's home counties.
site_illustration.rights_notes: Self-hosted. Generic illustration of the regiment cap badge, NOT a personal photo. military.html should add a one-line caveat. (Earlier hot-link path had become stale on Wikimedia.)

site_illustration.source: Geograph Britain & Ireland — image 575814, via Wikimedia Commons
site_illustration.license: WAS CC-BY-SA-2.0 (geograph) — file appears to have been DELETED from Wikimedia Commons
site_illustration.caption: Main Street, Portarlington — the small market town where the Greenes lived in the late 19th century.
site_illustration.rights_notes: BROKEN — Wikimedia returns 404 for both file page and image URL. Live site portarlington.html has been showing a broken image for some time. To be REPLACED by ill-portarlington-emo-park-c1900 (better period match anyway).

site_illustration.source: NLI Eason Photographic Collection, via Flickr Commons / Wikimedia Commons
site_illustration.license: PD-anonymous-EU (no known copyright restrictions per Flickr Commons)
site_illustration.caption: Emo Park, near Portarlington, c.1900-1920 — the kind of Edwardian estate landscape the Greenes would have known. (Eason Photographic Collection, NLI.)
site_illustration.credit: NLI Eason Photographic Collection (Public Domain) via Wikimedia Commons
site_illustration.rights_notes: Replaces broken ill-portarlington-main-street-geograph. Resolution: 2000x1425, 1.27MB. Better fit anyway — period (c.1900) rather than modern.

site_illustration.place: Holy Cross Church, Dundrum, Co. Dublin
site_illustration.source: Wikimedia Commons — File:Holy_Cross_Church_in_Dundrum.jpg, William Murphy via Flickr (infomatique)
site_illustration.caption: Holy Cross Catholic Church, Dundrum — where Thomas Greene and Bridget Clarke were married on 20 June 1922 (Phase 10 register entry 89).
site_illustration.rights_notes: Modern photo (2018). Building substantially as it was in 1922. Resolution 7952x5304, downloaded as 1280px wide variant.

site_illustration.source: Wikimedia Commons — File:Bekanntmachung_1942_deportation_Jersey.jpg, photographed by Wikimedia user Man vyi
site_illustration.license: PD (released to public domain by photographer; 1942 German military document long out of copyright)
site_illustration.caption: 1942 German Bekanntmachung (public notice) ordering deportation from Jersey to internment camps in Germany — the order that led to Elizabeth Harris being moved to Biberach (per family tradition).
site_illustration.credit: Photo: Man vyi / Wikimedia Commons (PD). Original notice: German occupation authorities, Jersey, 1942.
site_illustration.rights_notes: Primary-source historical document. Stronger illustration than a generic occupation photo because it directly depicts the mechanism by which Channel Islanders were deported.

hero_background.license: Unsplash Licence — free to use, attribution appreciated
hero_background.rights_notes: Generic mood image, not historical evidence. Acceptable for hero use.
Generic landscape — could be replaced in B2 with a c.1900 Lawrence Collection view of rural Co. Meath.
Could be replaced with a c.1900 Lawrence Collection view of Wellington Quay / O'Connell Bridge.
Generic — could be replaced with a Jersey/Channel Islands period view.
Used for both index hero and og:image meta-tag.
OpenGraph social-share preview image (1200px crop of the index hero).

process_artefacts.summary: 70+ PNG/HTML files under research-results/ are Playwright-captured search-form / results screenshots from the IG and Arolsen drivers. They are research process documentation, NOT gallery material. Most are gitignored (search-form, filled-form, results, final-state PNGs). They are intentionally excluded from this inventory.
kept_in_repo: register-p1.png and register.pdf for r1, r2, r3 (cited above as primary-evidence)

wishlist.description: Any family photographs Mark or Claire Garrigan hold — portraits, wedding photos, family groups, christenings, headstone photos, holiday snaps showing the older generations. Most likely to exist: photos of the next generation down (Mark's grandparents) plus possibly Bridget Clarke (b.1891, lived into the photographic era; almost certainly photographed in old age) and Elizabeth Harris (b.~1900, definitely lived into the photo era).
wishlist.action: Ask Mark to scan/photograph any albums in his possession or his sisters'/cousins' possession, OneDrive them to the .copilot-shared/ folder.

wishlist.description: Studio portraits or wedding photos of Thomas (b.~1870 — could have been photographed from c.1890), Bridget (b.1891 — wedding photo from 1922 would be in living-memory range for someone), Catherine (b.~1856 — studio portrait possible but unlikely for a labourer's widow).
wishlist.action: Long-shot. Check with extended Greene/Clarke cousins via family tree on Ancestry.com for tagged photos.

wishlist.description: If a Greene family member served in the Leinster Regiment (still unconfirmed but the regimental connection is suggested on military.html), a copy of the WW1 attestation paper would be primary evidence. Otherwise the Leinster cap-badge image stays as generic regimental illustration.
wishlist.action: Search ancestry.com Australian/UK service records, Findmypast Irish military records.

wishlist.description: Period (c.1880-1910) view of Portarlington Main Street, market square, French Church, train station. The Lawrence Collection at NLI is the canonical source.
wishlist.license_target: NLI Lawrence Collection (mostly out-of-copyright; check NLI catalogue for exact reuse terms)
wishlist.action: Search catalogue.nli.ie for 'Portarlington' in the Lawrence Photographic Collection.

wishlist.description: Period view of Duleek village, Mullaghfin townland (rural cottages c.1890s), or representative Co. Meath labourer's cottage. Lawrence Collection or Eason Collection at NLI.
wishlist.action: Search NLI for 'Duleek', 'Bellewstown', 'County Meath cottage'. Also check Meath Heritage Hub.

wishlist.priority: P1 — for dublin.html and the Bridget-as-Cook story
wishlist.description: View of Wellington Quay c.1910-1915 — where Joseph Dollard's printing house stood and where Bridget Clarke worked as Cook in 1911.
View of Rathmines or Ranelagh c.1900 (the Greene family's other Dublin connections lived in this area — Thomas at Clonliffe Avenue, Mountjoy in 1911).
Period photographs of Jersey under German occupation (1940-1945) and the 1942 deportation of British-born civilians to internment in Germany. The Jersey Heritage Trust archive has many; IWM has more.
wishlist.action: Browse jerseyheritage.org image library; supplement existing IWM Channel Islands liberation image with deportation/occupation context.

wishlist.place: Biberach an der Riß, Baden-Württemberg, Germany
wishlist.description: Period photograph of Ilag VB (the actual internment camp) rather than the modern town view we currently use.
wishlist.license_target: IWM, Wikimedia, or Channel Islands Occupation Society
wishlist.action: Search for 'Ilag V-B Biberach', 'Biberach internment camp', 'Channel Islands deportees Biberach'.

wishlist.description: The 1941 Jersey marriage certificate of Elizabeth Greene to Alfred Bower Harris. Referenced in past sessions as known-to-Mark but no scan in repo. Should be added.
wishlist.license_target: Jersey civil records (Crown © Bailiwick of Jersey; family copy permitted)
wishlist.action: Ask Mark for the scan; add to research-results/ev-1941-jersey-marriage/.

wishlist.description: Period image of Dollard's Printing House on Wellington Quay (Bridget's 1911 employer's premises). The firm operated for decades and was photographed.
wishlist.license_target: NLI / Dublin City Library and Archive
wishlist.action: Search for 'Dollard Printing House Wellington Quay' on catalogue.nli.ie and dublincity.ie.

wishlist.description: Photograph of the Roman Catholic Church of Holy Cross, Dundrum, Co. Dublin (where Thomas and Bridget married 20 June 1922).
wishlist.license_target: Wikimedia, Geograph, or modern photograph (CC-BY-SA preferred)
wishlist.action: Check Wikimedia 'Holy Cross Church Dundrum'; geograph.org.uk also has parish-church photos.

wishlist.description: Photograph (modern or historical) of Drummartin House, Dundrum — the Georgian country house where Bridget worked as housekeeper before her 1922 marriage.
wishlist.license_target: Wikimedia, Buildings of Ireland, or local heritage society
wishlist.action: Search dia.ie (Dictionary of Irish Architects), buildingsofireland.ie, and the South Dublin Libraries image archive.

wishlist.priority: P1 — primary evidence for the Clarke-grandparents hypothesis
wishlist.description: Photograph of the Thomas & Bridget Clarke (d.1877, d.1882) gravestone at Duleek Old Churchyard — currently we have only the Meath History Hub textual transcription. A photo would let us read it directly and look for further family names below the published transcription.
wishlist.license_target: Family-photographed (we'd take it ourselves on a visit) OR Meath History Hub if they hold one
wishlist.action: Email Meath History Hub; alternatively, schedule a Mark/Claire visit on next Ireland trip.
